config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society's interests should automatically come before individual rights, because once you give the collective a blank check, it is too easy to justify abuse, censorship, and unfair treatment of minorities. From my perspective, a healthy society protects individual rights first and then balances them only when there is a clear, limited public need. In Indonesia, I have seen how group pressure can be strong, so I am especially wary of sacrificing personal freedoms too easily.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that usually becomes a slogan for government overreach and the mistreatment of unpopular minorities. Public safety and the common good matter, but they have to be limited by constitutional rights, due process, and clear evidence that a restriction is necessary. If society can override individual rights whenever it claims a broad benefit, then those rights are not really rights at all. The better standard is that society can limit individual freedom only in narrow, justified cases, not as a default rule.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how appeals to harmony, order, or the common good can be used to pressure people into silence, and that can easily hurt minorities or anyone who does not fit the majority view. Society does matter, but it is strongest when individual rights are protected first and limits are applied only when there is a clear, necessary, and fair public reason. If the collective always wins by default, then personal freedom becomes too fragile to trust.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that is a fast track to government overreach and abuse of unpopular people. I do think society sometimes has to limit individual freedom for real public needs like safety, disease control, or preventing harm, but those limits need strict justification, due process, and narrow scope. If "the common good" can override rights whenever it is convenient, then rights are just permissions the majority hands out and can take back. In my view, a free society protects individual rights first and only narrows them when the public need is concrete, evidence-based, and unavoidable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority by default, because in Indonesia I have seen how “harmony” and “public order” can become excuses to silence dissent and pressure minorities. Society does matter, but it is not healthy when the collective gets a free pass to override rights whenever it claims a noble goal. I would only accept limits on individual rights when the public need is clear, necessary, and tightly constrained, not just because the majority feels uncomfortable. If rights can be sacrificed too easily, then they are not real protections at all.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual, at least not as a general rule. From my American perspective, that kind of idea is too easy to turn into government overreach, mob rule, or an excuse to target unpopular people in the name of the common good. Society absolutely can impose limited restrictions for real needs like public safety or disease control, but those limits should be narrow, evidence-based, and protected by due process. If individual rights can be overridden whenever the majority says it is for society's benefit, then those rights are basically meaningless.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
